name: Kupalnitsa
description: Kupalnitsa, also known as Kupala, is a Slavic goddess associated with summer solstice, love, fertility, herbs, and purification. She is celebrated during Kupala Night (Noc Kupały), a festival filled with bonfires, rituals, and herbal magic. Her origins are intertwined with pre-Christian Slavic beliefs, blended with later Christian influences which resulted in the association with St. John the Baptist (Ivan Kupala). Kupalnitsa's power lies in her ability to bring abundance, cleanse negativity, and ignite passion. She is believed to bless unions, ensure fruitful harvests, and protect against evil spirits. During Kupala Night, rituals involving fire and water are performed to honor her and invoke her blessings. Young couples leap over bonfires for good luck and love, while others search for the magical fern flower, believed to bloom only on this night and grant wishes. Kupalnitsa represents the peak of summer's energy, a time of growth, renewal, and celebration of life's vital forces. She embodies the feminine power of nature and the cyclical rhythm of the seasons. She is sometimes paired with Kupalo, a male deity representing the sun and fertility, symbolizing the union of masculine and feminine energies. Her importance is reflected in the enduring traditions and folklore surrounding Kupala Night, which continues to be celebrated in various forms throughout Slavic countries.
subtitle: Goddess of Summer Solstice and Fertility

keyMyths.title: The Fern Flower
keyMyths.description: Legend says a magical fern flower blooms only on Kupala Night, granting wishes and revealing hidden treasures to those who find it. Finding the fern flower is a symbol of luck and spiritual enlightenment.

keyMyths.title: Leaping Over the Bonfire
keyMyths.description: Couples leap over bonfires during Kupala Night to purify themselves, ensure a prosperous future, and test the strength of their love. If they leap successfully, their relationship will be blessed.

keyMyths.title: Herbal Magic
keyMyths.description: Herbs collected on Kupala Night are believed to possess heightened magical properties, used for healing, love spells, and protection against evil spirits. St. John's Wort is especially significant.

sources.author: Zbigniew Gołąb
sources.work: The Origins of the Slavs: A Linguist's View
sources.description: Discusses the linguistic roots and possible origins of Slavic deities and beliefs.
sources.text: Discusses the linguistic roots and possible origins of Slavic deities and beliefs.

sources.author: Georges Dumézil
sources.description: Comparative analysis of Indo-European mythologies, offering insights into potential connections and influences on Slavic beliefs.

sources.author: Maria Gimbutas
sources.description: Explores the role of goddesses in ancient European cultures, providing context for understanding Kupalnitsa's significance.
